text: Hi Tyler! Welcome! And I do have a question: What do you think is the number 1 impediment to developer productivity for most people such that once removed, they're much more effective? Asked another, what's the low-hanging fruit in your experience for most (maybe not necessarily as seasoned as you are) developers?

text: Jef Raskin sure loved the idea of ZUIs (Humane Interface ch.6), but I don't know that the core idea, that our spatial awareness would carry over into a 2D window, was ever really proven out.  It hadn't yet been when he first wrote about them, at least.

text: From my POV, one of the big ones is getting better with tools.  You should be able to navigate to any function in your code with a few keystrokes/bring up any file.  You should be able to do complex editing easily without reaching for the mouse.  You should have a project that you can edit/launch/debug in seconds.  Those things are important and often get overlooked.

Those things are important and often get overlooked.

[May 5th, 2020 4:12 AM] g.hatsevich: Maybe it is a good idea to use zooming (ctrl + mouse wheel) to transition between various visual representations of the program. Zoom out — and you'll see the whole picture. Zoom in — and you'll see the code. Zoom in even further — and you'll see some details. Etc. I wonder how many different useful layers in between we can think of.

Are there programming environments which somehow implement this idea?
Do you like this idea?

text:
Zooming is used a fair amount nowadays. You see it in the Atom editor, with the shrunken form of the document on the right. It is used extensively in Fancade, which because it is based on a Isometric projection of 3D space, is quite natural. All graphic design products use zooming constantly. The reason you don't see it used that much in programming is that once you zoom text below 6 pt it is illegible, and therefore useless. Seeing the shape of code does not inform you in any way as to the function of the code. Unlike a bitmap which can be sampled to 1/16th of its pixels and still be quite legible, code basically turns to mush when shrunken. Looking at a blobby bunch of wires may be sexy at first glance, but without comprehension it is mere graphical artifice with no substance or actual productive value. I don't find the Atom shrunken text form on the right is much better than a scrollbar.

Zooming does preserve context better than jumping, and i think we will see more of it, but don't pin your hopes on a UI trick going to advance programming significantly.

text: How much learning curve are people usually willing to put up with for a new productivity tool? I never learnt vim (properly, I can edit and save) because it always felt like an unnecessarily large investment for that particular task. If I took all those tasks together, and all future tasks, almost any learning curve is probably worth it.

elements.text: How much learning curve are people usually willing to put up with for a new productivity tool? I never learnt vim (properly, I can edit and save) because it always felt like an unnecessarily large investment for that particular task. If I took all those tasks together, and all future tasks, almost any learning curve is probably worth it.

text:
<@UT9TWSZB5> Bolierplate is a tough one, especially for things like C++.  There is always setup/config/makefile time that is tedious and error prone.  Building starter projects and libraries of common pieces is a good approach to that.
<@UDQBTJ211> Not to be a Vim zealot, but learning it was a turning point for me.  I tried a couple of times, but when I finally put in the effort I think I became much more productive.  Part of that was just mindset.  I realized that if I could be so much better just editing text, what else was I missing?  That led me down the path of figuring out jumplists, tagging, file search, etc.  I did a lot of that stuff before, but Vim taught me that there is often a better way if you look for it.  I wasted a lot of years reaching for the mouse.....

elements.text: Bolierplate is a tough one, especially for things like C++.  There is always setup/config/makefile time that is tedious and error prone.  Building starter projects and libraries of common pieces is a good approach to that.

Not to be a Vim zealot, but learning it was a turning point for me.  I tried a couple of times, but when I finally put in the effort I think I became much more productive.  Part of that was just mindset.  I realized that if I could be so much better just editing text, what else was I missing?  That led me down the path of figuring out jumplists, tagging, file search, etc.  I did a lot of that stuff before, but Vim taught me that there is often a better way if you look for it.  I wasted a lot of years reaching for the mouse.....

text:
<@UK69SS8LB>: Exchanging ideas with other productive people for sure. “Exchanging” doesn't have to be bidirectional though, even reading the writings of productive people helps a lot. 
The lowest hanging fruit in my experience is reading this book: <http://www.catb.org/esr/writings/taoup/html/|http://www.catb.org/esr/writings/taoup/html/>. The original unix developers are some of the most productive programmers I've ever seen, in large part because of their design skills.
I agree with Chris about the importance of proficiency with tools and I'll add also the importance of making your own tools.

<@UT9TWSZB5>: I'm not sure what tradeoff you're talking about. Boilerplate code is rather minimal in the big picture of productivity in my experience, there's much bigger game to hunt. 
Developer tooling has its current variety because software is used in a variety of environments. What works well for one situation might not work so well for another.

<@UDQBTJ211>: Most people aren't willing to put in any more time than they absolutely must. If they need to learn a tool for a grade or a job or a project, then they'll do it. But learning a tool or technique just because it seems like it'll pay off? That's rare.

text: <@UL5AX4G2H> there’s usually a tension between having some logic that is simple to use with a default functionality, but then there are endless configuration options. Or, the same logic could be composed of 20 different pieces that requires some boilerplate for the default functionality but then also let’s you change the functionality directly by how the pieces compose vs a bunch of configuration options.

elements.text: there’s usually a tension between having some logic that is simple to use with a default functionality, but then there are endless configuration options. Or, the same logic could be composed of 20 different pieces that requires some boilerplate for the default functionality but then also let’s you change the functionality directly by how the pieces compose vs a bunch of configuration options.

text:
I think that scrollbars make the most sense for documents that scroll in 1 dimension. For moving a viewport across a large 2D canvas, I prefer a UI that lets you pan in 2D using mouse movements. Like Google Maps, for example. Using two separate X and Y scrollbars to move around is clumsy by comparison. If I want to visualize where my viewport is in 2D space, I prefer a 2D map of the entire canvas that shows my current viewport position, rather than 2 separate X and Y scroll bars. If you add zooming to this (a Z axis), then again I like the Google Maps UI where you use the mouse scroll wheel or the trackpad scroll gesture to zoom in and out, in preference to a Z axis scroll bar. This is what I've mostly implemented in my Curv project.

Curv is missing a "time scrollbar" for rewinding an animation, but I think this is a good idea. It works well in the viewer for Youtube videos.

text: <@UT9TWSZB5> Config (with defaults) is used to enable a tool to solve a bounded problem easily. Composition, which may include boiler plate, is used to enable a collection of tools to solve unbounded problems. A unix pipeline is a good example of managing the tension properly. Each step in the pipeline is a command with configurable options that easily solves a bounded problem (ex grep finds lines matching a regex), and unix itself permits composition to solve an unbounded problem (a pipeline runs an arbitrary program).

elements.text: Config (with defaults) is used to enable a tool to solve a bounded problem easily. Composition, which may include boiler plate, is used to enable a collection of tools to solve unbounded problems. A unix pipeline is a good example of managing the tension properly. Each step in the pipeline is a command with configurable options that easily solves a bounded problem (ex grep finds lines matching a regex), and unix itself permits composition to solve an unbounded problem (a pipeline runs an arbitrary program).

text:
Are you looking to communicate why a dependency exists? I'd think using a directed graph to visualize dependencies and then having a reference from the edge to a long text blurb would be pretty good. So the graph might be A --> B to mean A depends on B, and then --> is clickable url to a new page with a description of why A depends on B

Do you mind elaborating on what you mean by "what you could expect"? An example would help me a lot

text:
I'm not exactly sure. I feel like I don't know what I don't know. I think it's about not knowing _what_ is possible to use as a notation for documentation. For example, I am working on an application right now that uses an Entity Component System to manage "business logic" and as such, there are multiple representations of the same data that are possible, the "Serialized model" which is shared with other systems, and the live model which lives within the component storages. What is a good way to document these different ways that we manage the same data? Similarly, some applications have a "Domain model", "Database model", and an "API Model" for the same concept like a "Delivery Route", do notations exist that can quickly communicate the relationship between the "Delivery Route" used in the database, versus in the Domain model, versus the API Model? I'm not sure...
I feel like it's an "unknown unknown"–Like I don't know that something exists, therefore I don't know how to look for it.

elements.text: I'm not exactly sure. I feel like I don't know what I don't know. I think it's about not knowing
what

is possible to use as a notation for documentation. For example, I am working on an application right now that uses an Entity Component System to manage "business logic" and as such, there are multiple representations of the same data that are possible, the "Serialized model" which is shared with other systems, and the live model which lives within the component storages. What is a good way to document these different ways that we manage the same data? Similarly, some applications have a "Domain model", "Database model", and an "API Model" for the same concept like a "Delivery Route", do notations exist that can quickly communicate the relationship between the "Delivery Route" used in the database, versus in the Domain model, versus the API Model? I'm not sure...
I feel like it's an "unknown unknown"–Like I don't know that something exists, therefore I don't know how to look for it.
